title: CIL System — Advanced Mock 15 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of Japan?
questions.solution: The Yen is the currency of Japan; Yuan is Chinese, Won is Korean.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of the United Kingdom?
questions.solution: The UK's currency is the Pound Sterling (£).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where are the headquarters of the United Nations located?
questions.solution: The UN headquarters is located in New York City, USA.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where is the headquarters of the World Health Organization (WHO)?
questions.solution: The WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is India's national space agency?
questions.solution: The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is India's space agency.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Taj Mahal is located in which city?
questions.solution: The Taj Mahal is in Agra, Uttar Pradesh, on the bank of the Yamuna.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Sun Temple at Konark is located in which state?
questions.solution: The Konark Sun Temple is in Odisha, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In which year did the Dandi (Salt) March take place?
questions.solution: Gandhi led the Dandi Salt March in 1930 during the Civil Disobedience Movement.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Quit India Movement was launched in which year?
questions.solution: The Quit India Movement began in August 1942.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who founded the Maurya Empire?
questions.solution: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 321 BCE.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: How many players are there in a cricket team on the field?
questions.solution: A cricket team fields eleven players.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Thomas Cup is associated with which sport?
questions.solution: The Thomas Cup is the premier men's international team badminton event.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Bharat Ratna is India's highest:
questions.solution: The Bharat Ratna is the highest civilian award of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution regulates monetary policy in India?
questions.solution: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is the country's central bank and monetary authority.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: GST in India was implemented from which date?
questions.solution: The Goods and Services Tax came into force on 1 July 2017.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 4 h, but with a leak it takes 6 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 4 h; with leak b = 6 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{4}-\tfrac1{6}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{4\times6}{6-4}$.
**Answer.** $T=12$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 20000 at 8% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 20000, r = 8% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=20000\left(\tfrac{8}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 128.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 120 m and 160 m long move in opposite directions at 54 km/h and 48 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 120 m, L₂ = 160 m, s₁ = 54 km/h, s₂ = 48 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(54+48)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=28.33$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{120+160}{28.33}$.
**Answer.** $t=9.88$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 5000 for 8 months and B invests Rs 8000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 6000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 5000×8 mo; B: Rs 8000×6 mo; profit Rs 6000.
**Working.** Ratio $=40000:48000$; share$_A=6000\times\tfrac{40000}{88000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 2727.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 8:10 is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The hour hand sweeps 30° per hour and 0.5° per minute, the minute hand 6° per minute; take the smaller of the two angles.
**Formula.** $\theta=\left|30H-5.5M\right|$
**Given.** H = 8, M = 10.
**Working.** $=\left|30\times8-5.5\times10\right|=185^\circ$; smaller $=\min(185,360-185)$.
**Answer.** $\theta=175^\circ$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat covers 77 km downstream in 7 h and the same 77 km upstream in 11 h. Its speed in still water is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Downstream speed adds the stream; upstream subtracts it. Still-water speed is the average of the two.
**Formula.** $u=\tfrac12\left[(b+s)+(b-s)\right]$
**Given.** Downstream 77 km in 7 h; upstream 77 km in 11 h.
**Working.** Downstream $=\tfrac{77}{7}=11$ km/h, upstream $=\tfrac{77}{11}=7$ km/h.
**Answer.** Still-water speed $u=\tfrac{11+7}{2}=9$ km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 40% above cost, then gives a 20% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 40%, discount d = 20%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{40}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{20}{100}\right)=1.12$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=12\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 80 L of milk. 20 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 3 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 80 L, x = 20 L, n = 3.
**Working.** $=80\left(1-\tfrac{20}{80}\right)^{3}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 33.75$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 5 h, but with a leak it takes 7 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 5 h; with leak b = 7 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{5}-\tfrac1{7}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{5\times7}{7-5}$.
**Answer.** $T=17.5$ h.

questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 8 h and pipe B in 15 h. Together they fill it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The probability of getting a prime number on a single throw of a fair die is:
questions.solution: Count the favourable outcomes out of 6 equally likely faces to get 1/2.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The compound ratio of 3 : 5 and 5 : 9 is:
questions.solution: Compound ratio = (3×5) : (5×9) = 15 : 45.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A and B invest in the ratio 4 : 5. Out of a total profit of Rs 13500, A's share is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 12 men complete a work in 18 days, 6 men will complete it in:
questions.solution: Men-days constant: 12×18 = 6×D, D = 36 days.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 121 is divided in the ratio 4 : 7, the larger part is:
questions.solution: Larger part = 121 × 7/11 = 77.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Compound interest on Rs 8000 at 8% per annum for 2 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A man goes at 30 km/h and returns over the same route at 90 km/h. His average speed is:
questions.solution: Average speed = 2uv/(u+v) = 2×30×90/(30+90) = 45 km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 25% of a number is 60, the number is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A car covers a distance at 72 km/h in 6 hours. The distance is:
questions.solution: Distance = speed × time = 72 × 6 = 432 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A can do a work in 15 days and B in 24 days. Working together they finish it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A sum doubles itself in 20 years under simple interest. The annual rate is:
questions.solution: To double, SI = P, so rate = 100/T = 100/20 = 5%.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A 150 m long train crosses a pole in 20 seconds. Its speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A number divided by 9 gives quotient 13 and remainder 4. The number is:
questions.solution: Number = divisor × quotient + remainder = 9×13+4 = 121.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An article sold at Rs 600 incurs a 25% loss. Its cost price is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An item bought for Rs 1000 is sold for Rs 1120. The profit percentage is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: X is the husband of Y. Z is the mother of Y. How is Z related to X?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Mother-in-law.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: No A is B. All B are C. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: Some C are not A.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 5 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(5² + 12²) = √169 = 13 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'BOOK' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: BOOK → DQQM.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 5, 11, 23, 47, 95, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 95 × 2 + 1 = 191.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'GEM'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of G, E, M to get 25.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the brother of B. B is the brother of C. C is the father of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Uncle.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All pens are books. All books are tables. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: All pens are tables.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: N, O, P, Q, ?
questions.solution: Consecutive alphabet letters; after Q comes R.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 3, 6, 12, 21, ?
questions.solution: Differences grow by 3; adding the next difference gives 33.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: The angle between the hands of a clock at 2:00 is:
questions.solution: Each hour = 30°; at 2:00 the hands are 2 × 30 = 60° apart.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: 1, 8, 20, 27?
questions.solution: The others are perfect cubes; 20 is not.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 12 → 6.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A person facing west turns 90° to the left. Now they face:
questions.solution: Turning left from west leads to South.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Hand : Glove :: Foot : ?
questions.solution: Just as Glove relates to Hand, Sock relates to Foot.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A=1, B=2, ... then the sum of the letters of CAB is:
questions.solution: Add the alphabet positions of C, A, B to get 6.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 18 people, X is 7th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 18 − 7 + 1 = 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: The only daughter of her mother is the woman herself, so she is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: 2 : 8 :: 4 : ? (pattern n : n^3)

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 2, 4, 8, 16, ?
questions.solution: Each term is multiplied by 2, so next = 16 × 2 = 32.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 9, 16, 25, 36, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 7² = 49.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: Z, X, V, T, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is R.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: Copper, Iron, Oxygen, Zinc?
questions.solution: Oxygen belongs to a different category from the rest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: AB, DE, GH, ?
questions.solution: Each pair skips one letter; after GH comes JK.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'a piece of cake' means:
questions.solution: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.solution: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happening very rarely.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to bury the hatchet' means:
questions.solution: 'Bury the hatchet' means to end a quarrel and make peace.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.solution: It means to accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Child' is:
questions.solution: 'Child' has the irregular plural 'children'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Mouse' is:
questions.solution: 'Mouse' has the irregular plural 'mice'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Neither he nor I ___ wrong.'
questions.solution: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearer subject 'I', so 'am'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Each of the students ___ a book.'
questions.solution: 'Each' is singular, so it takes 'has'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is ___ honest man.'
questions.solution: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is used.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.solution: Musical instruments take the definite article 'the'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'John wrote the letter.' is:
options: The letter was written by John
The letter is written by John
questions.solution: Past simple active becomes 'was written' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'She is reading a book.' is:
options: A book is being read by her
A book has been read by her
A book is read by her
A book was read by her
questions.solution: Present continuous active becomes 'is being read' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'He / don't / like / coffee.'
questions.solution: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'One of the boys / are / playing / outside.'
questions.solution: 'One of the boys' is singular, so it requires 'is'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'I ___ my homework already.'
questions.solution: 'Already' signals present perfect, so 'have done' is correct.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By next year, she ___ here for a decade.'
questions.solution: A completed action by a future point uses the future perfect 'will have worked'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the word that means the opposite of 'Artificial'.
questions.solution: 'Artificial' is opposite to 'natural'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct meaning of the word 'Frugal'.
questions.solution: 'Frugal' means careful with money, i.e. economical.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Abundant'.
questions.solution: 'Abundant' means existing in large quantity, i.e. plentiful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Brief'.
questions.solution: 'Brief' means short or concise.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Happy'.
questions.solution: 'Happy' is synonymous with 'joyful'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Enormous'.
questions.solution: 'Enormous' means very large, i.e. huge.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Diligent'.
questions.solution: 'Diligent' describes someone hard-working and careful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Candid'.
questions.solution: 'Candid' means open and frank in speech.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Vivid'.
questions.solution: 'Vivid' means bright, clear and intense.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average-case time to search a well-distributed hash table is:
questions.solution: With a good hash function and low load factor, lookup is expected constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For every nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) there exists:
questions.solution: NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same (regular) languages — the subset construction proves it.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A max-heap is a complete binary tree in which each parent is:
options: Greater than or equal to its children
questions.solution: In a max-heap every parent ≥ its children, putting the maximum at the root.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 2F₁₆ is:
questions.solution: 2F₁₆ = 47₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which condition is NOT one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock needs mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, NO preemption, and circular wait — so 'preemption' is not a condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Given the network 192.168.1.0/26, the number of usable host addresses per subnet is:
questions.solution: /26 leaves 6 host bits ⇒ 2⁶ − 2 = 62 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 2K memory locations?
questions.solution: 2K = 2048 = 2^11, so 11 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For large n, which growth ordering is correct?
options: log n grows faster than n
questions.solution: Exponential 2ⁿ eventually dominates any polynomial like n².

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 3 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^3 = 8; host bits = 5 ⇒ 2^5 − 2 = 30 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 128 elements needs at most how many comparisons?
questions.solution: Worst-case comparisons ≈ ⌈log₂ 128⌉ = 7.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as AND.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 140" width="220" height="140"><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="40" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="160" y1="30" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="110" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">A</text><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">B</text><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">C</text><circle cx="160" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">D</text></svg>
figure.caption: Undirected graph (4 nodes)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
questions.solution: ACID guarantees reliable transaction processing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 16K memory locations?
questions.solution: 16K = 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: AND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(7) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 21, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about a singly linked list is correct?
options: It does not support O(1) random access by index
Its size must be fixed at creation
questions.solution: Linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach an arbitrary index — no random access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 2 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 2KB = 2^11 bytes, so the offset field is 11 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: AND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block can be placed in:
questions.solution: Direct mapping fixes each block to one line (block mod #lines), unlike associative caches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 5 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 5×4/2 = 10 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up both reads and writes equally
Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 6-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 6 bits give 2^6 = 64 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for on-disk databases because they:
options: Are shallow (high fan-out), reducing disk accesses
questions.solution: High branching factor keeps B-trees shallow, minimizing expensive disk seeks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 1010₂ is:
questions.solution: 1010₂ = 10₁₀ (sum of place values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear search of an unsorted array of n items takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: It may examine every element, so worst-case time is O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 6-stage pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about greedy algorithms is correct?
options: They are the same as dynamic programming
They do not always yield a globally optimal solution
questions.solution: Greedy choices are optimal only for problems with the greedy-choice property (e.g. MST); not in general.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The base (radix) of the hexadecimal number system is:
questions.solution: Hexadecimal uses 16 symbols (0–9, A–F).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 8, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths correctly only when edge weights are:
questions.solution: Dijkstra assumes non-negative weights; negative edges require Bellman–Ford.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×20 array of 4-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 200×20×4 = 16000 B = 15.63 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 6 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 6×5/2 = 15 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 8, push 20, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Has its own copy of global data
Has a completely separate address space
Shares the address space of its process
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 100 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 1Mbps×100ms ÷ 8 ≈ 12.21 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:
questions.solution: HTTP uses port 80; HTTPS uses 443.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/250 = 0.4 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about virtual memory is INCORRECT?
options: Virtual memory can exceed physical RAM
Page faults are handled entirely by the CPU without disk access
A page fault may load a page from disk
The page table maps pages to frames
questions.solution: A page fault for a non-resident page requires the OS to fetch it from disk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(5) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 8, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP connection is established using a:
questions.solution: TCP setup uses SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; teardown uses a four-way exchange.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 50 slots holds 75 keys. Its load factor is:
questions.solution: Load factor α = n/m = 75/50 = 1.5.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) guarantees:
questions.solution: 2PL (growing then shrinking phase) ensures serializability, though deadlocks are still possible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The adjacency-matrix representation of a graph with V vertices needs space:
questions.solution: A V×V matrix stores every possible edge, using O(V²) space regardless of edge count.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 6 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly — more frames causing more page faults — can occur with which replacement policy?
questions.solution: FIFO can exhibit Belady's anomaly; stack algorithms like LRU and OPT cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 8 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.002×8ms ≈ 16099.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: IP addresses at the network layer
MAC addresses at the data-link layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /26 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 26 = 6; usable = 2^6 − 2 = 62.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: With consistently poor pivots (e.g. already-sorted input) quicksort degrades to O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">OR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: OR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average and worst-case time complexity of merge sort is:
questions.solution: Merge sort always divides and merges in O(n log n), and it is stable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In 8-bit two's complement, the range of representable integers is:
questions.solution: 8-bit two's complement spans −2⁷ to 2⁷ − 1 = −128 to +127.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 8-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 8 bits give 2^8 = 256 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 10000010 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 130 − 256 = -126.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 32 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 32 bits ÷ 8 = 4 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bubble sort has a worst-case time complexity of:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes ~n²/2 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 6-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Linear search works on unsorted arrays
Binary search runs in O(log n)
Binary search works on an unsorted array
Binary search requires a sorted array
questions.solution: Binary search only works on sorted data — the claim that it works unsorted is false.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 60), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 32, 40, 48, 60, 80.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">60</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">80</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">32</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">48</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 33 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Translates the whole program before execution
Executes line by line at run time
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of n elements runs in:
questions.solution: Each comparison halves the search space, giving logarithmic time — but the array must be sorted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 8, push 14, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing compilation and interpretation is correct?
options: Compiled code generally runs faster than interpreted code
questions.solution: Ahead-of-time compiled native code typically executes faster than line-by-line interpretation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a 5-stage instruction pipeline, a data hazard arises when:
options: An instruction needs a result not yet produced by an earlier instruction
questions.solution: Data hazards occur from dependencies; they are mitigated by forwarding or stalls.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 50), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 22, 30, 38, 50, 75.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">75</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">22</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">38</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree
